cards.text: A {{c1::dependency}} occurs when one object invokes another object's functionality in order to accomplish some {{c2::specific task}}.

cards.front: How does a dependency differ from an association in terms of relationship strength?
cards.back: A dependency is a weaker relationship than an association. The dependent object only needs the other object for a specific task, not as an ongoing relationship.

cards.front: What is the directionality of a dependency relationship?
cards.back: A dependency is always a unidirectional relationship - one object depends on another, but not vice versa.

cards.text: Dependencies typically are {{c1::not members}}. Rather, the object being depended on is typically {{c2::instantiated as needed}} or {{c3::passed into a function as a parameter}}.

cards.question: What type of relationship does Point have with std::ostream?
cards.explanation: Point has a dependency on std::ostream. It uses std::ostream to accomplish the specific task of printing, but std::ostream is not a member of Point and is passed in as a parameter.

cards.front: What is the key difference between a dependency and an association in C++?
cards.back: Associations keep a direct or indirect link to the associated class as a member. Dependencies do not have the depended-on object as a member - it's instantiated as needed or passed as a parameter.
Dependency relationship verb - indicates one object depends on another to perform a specific task

cards.text: In a dependency, the relationship verb is {{c1::depends-on}}, and the relationship type is {{c2::otherwise unrelated}}.

cards.front: What is a common example of a dependency that you use frequently in C++?
cards.back: std::ostream (std::cout) - classes use it to accomplish the task of printing to the console, but it's not a member and is only used when needed.

cards.front: Even though dependency is the weakest relationship, why can it still be problematic?
cards.back: Any change to the object being depended upon may break functionality in the dependent caller, so there is still coupling between the objects.

cards.text: A dependency is a {{c1::weaker}} relationship than association, and is always {{c2::unidirectional}}.
In a dependency, the depended-on object is typically {{c1::instantiated as needed}} or {{c2::passed as a parameter}}, not stored as a member.
